title: Photon Momentum Enabled Symmetry Breaking and Nonlinear Photocurrents in the Centrosymmetric Dirac Semimetal PdTe
authors: Sambhu G Nath, Subhadip Manna, R K Gopal, Chiranjib Mitra
abstract: In centrosymmetric Dirac semimetals, second order nonlinear photocurrents are forbidden by the coexistence of time-reversal and inversion symmetries. Here, we demonstrate that finite photon momentum transfer acts as a dynamic symmetry breaking mechanism in PdTe, enabling nonlinear optical responses that are nominally forbidden in the centrosymmetric bulk. Through polarization sensitive measurements, we resolve distinct contributions from the circular photogalvanic effect (CPGE), geometric shift currents, and photon drag mediated processes. We show that the helicity dependent current vanishes at normal incidence and reverses sign with the angle of incidence, reflecting the coupling between photons and spin polarized surface states. Crucially, thickness dependent analysis reveals that the helicity dependent photocurrent component C scales with film thickness, establishing a robust bulk contribution enabled by momentum transfer. This confirms that incident photons provide the directional axis required to probe interband quantum geometry, rather than the response originating solely from surface states or strain. Our results demonstrate that optical excitation can dynamically reduce the effective symmetry of the system, enabling access to quantum geometric tensors and establishing PdTe as a promising platform for exploring nonequilibrium dynamics governed by photon momentum in high symmetry topological materials.
summary: This paper demonstrates that the momentum of incident photons can dynamically break the inversion symmetry of a centrosymmetric Dirac semimetal. This allows for the observation of previously forbidden nonlinear optical effects, providing a new way to probe quantum geometric properties in topological materials.
main_problem: In centrosymmetric Dirac semimetals, second-order nonlinear photocurrents are forbidden by time-reversal and inversion symmetries. The study investigates how photon momentum transfer can act as a dynamic symmetry-breaking mechanism to enable these forbidden responses.
main_result: The researchers demonstrate that finite photon momentum transfer in PdTe enables nonlinear optical responses, including a helicity-dependent current that scales with film thickness, proving a robust bulk contribution rather than just surface effects.
method: The study uses polarization-resolved photocurrent measurements with a continuous wave laser, varying the incident angle and polarization state (using a quarter-wave plate) to resolve different current components.
model_or_system: The physical system is PdTe, a centrosymmetric Dirac semimetal, prepared as thin films deposited on Al2O3 substrates via Pulsed Laser Deposition.
key_observables: Transverse photocurrent density (Jy), helicity-dependent component (C), and helicity-independent components (L1, L2).
important_parameters: Incident angle (theta), polarization angle (alpha), film thickness, and laser power.
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes the observed nonlinearities are driven by photon momentum transfer rather than intrinsic structural asymmetry or strain-induced symmetry breaking.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows the experimental geometry; Fig 2 displays normalized photocurrent density and polar plots vs polarization angle; Fig 4 shows polarization-dependent modulation and extracted components; Fig 5 shows power and thickness dependence; Fig 6 and 7 show HWP rotation and angular dependence.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the symmetry problem, describes the experimental setup and PdTe film growth, presents polarization-resolved measurements to extract current components, performs thickness and angular dependence analysis to distinguish bulk from surface effects, and concludes with potential applications.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Schematic illustration of the experimental geometry, showing the directions of the incident laser beam and the measured photocurrent with respect to the sample normal.

FIG. 2. (a,b) Photocurrent density Jy, normalized by the incident light intensity, as a function of the quarter-wave plate rotation angle α for selected negative (a) and positive (b) angles of incidence θ relative to the sample normal. The corresponding polarization states of the incident light for different values of α are indicated on the top axis. (c,d) Polar plots of the same data shown in panels (c) and (d), respectively. Solid lines represent fits to Eq.(1), as discussed in the main text.

FIG. 3. Angle of incidence (θ) dependence of (a) C, (b) L1, (c) L2, and (d) D. The solid curves denote fits to the corresponding functional forms indicated in each panel, with the extracted fitting parameters shown alongside.

FIG. 4. (a) Polarization dependent modulation of the photocurrent measured at different values of ϕ for a fixed angle of incidence θ = −50◦. (b) Polar representation of the same data shown in panel (a). Solid lines represent fits to Eq.(1), as discussed in the main text. (c) Comparison of the extracted photocurrent components C, L1, and L2 of Jy for two different values of ϕ.

FIG. 5. (a) Photocurrent Jy, measured at different incident laser powers, plotted as a function of the quarter-wave plate (QWP) rotation angle α. (b)Dependence of the photocurrent components C, L1, L2, and D on the average incident laser power. All components exhibit linear behavior in the power range of 0-40 mW, as indicated by the solid-line fits. (c) Thickness dependence of the helicity dependent component of photocurrent (C).
